session_hint: the Redis cache hit-rate collapse investigation
question: What did we conclude was causing the Redis cache hit-rate to collapse?
turns: user
Our Redis cache hit rate dropped from 92% to 41% overnight. Starting investigation. No code was deployed in the last 48 hours.
user
Checked Redis slow log — a handful of Lua scripts are taking 8ms each, but they fire maybe once per minute. Probably not the cause given the volume of misses.
user
Pulled network stats between app servers and Redis: average latency is 0.4ms, zero packet drops reported by the NIC. Network looks clean.
user
Examined key TTL settings across our hot keyspace — product catalog keys all have 1h TTLs, session keys 24h, unchanged for months. TTL expiry is not the cause.
user

Found it: maxmemory-policy was changed to allkeys-lru last Tuesday during a capacity review. maxmemory is set to 2GB. Memory usage crossed 1.9GB just after midnight and Redis started evicting hot keys under LRU pressure. That's the root cause — allkeys-lru eviction under memory pressure wiped the hot keyspace.
user
Verified by checking evicted_keys counter in Redis INFO stats: 214,000 keys evicted between midnight and 2am. Product catalog keys were the primary casualty given their size.
user
Bumped Redis maxmemory to 4GB as immediate mitigation. Cache hit rate climbing back — now at 78% and rising.
user
Added a CloudWatch alarm on Redis UsedMemory > 80% of maxmemory so we catch this before eviction starts next time.
user
Revisited Lua scripts: they are infrequent and short-lived, confirmed no impact on hit rate. Closing that angle.
user
Changing maxmemory-policy back to volatile-lru so only keys with explicit TTLs are eviction candidates. Much safer default. Incident closed.
